key_points: Coordinate geometry provides a numerical method to represent geometric figures
The Cartesian coordinate system was introduced by René Descartes
The coordinate plane is formed by two perpendicular number lines intersecting at the origin
Coordinates of a point describe its position relative to both axes
The first coordinate represents horizontal displacement while the second represents vertical displacement
Points with both positive coordinates lie in the first quadrant
Points with negative x and positive y lie in the second quadrant
Points with both coordinates negative lie in the third quadrant
Points with positive x and negative y lie in the fourth quadrant
Understanding coordinate positions helps interpret graphs and geometric relationships
examples: The point (2,5) lies in the first quadrant
The point (-4,7) lies in the second quadrant
The point (-6,-3) lies in the third quadrant
The point (8,-2) lies in the fourth quadrant
The point (0,-5) lies on the negative y-axis
The point (-7,0) lies on the negative x-axis
exam_tips: Visualize the coordinate plane before determining the quadrant
Carefully identify sign combinations of coordinates
Points on axes should be recognized quickly to avoid quadrant errors
Use clear graph plotting for better understanding of coordinate positions
Remember that the origin divides the axes into positive and negative directions
